title: Psalms 120
subtitle: I Call on the LORD in My Distress

heb.gloss: song of ascents / song of the steps
heb.paragraph: "A song of ascents (hammaʿaălōṭ)" (superscription). The first of fifteen Songs of Ascents (120–134), sung by pilgrims ascending to Jerusalem for the three annual festivals. Psalm 120 is the pilgrim’s starting point — still in exile, longing for Zion. "I call on the LORD in my distress, and he answers me" (v.1). "Save me, LORD, from lying lips and from deceitful tongues" (v.2). The deceitful tongue is compared to a warrior’s sharp arrows and burning coals (vv.3–4).

notes.note: "I call on the LORD in my distress. Save me from lying lips." The pilgrimage begins in distress and deception. The journey to Zion is an escape from lies.
"What will he do to you, you deceitful tongue? He will punish you with a warrior’s sharp arrows, with burning coals." Arrows and fire: the penalties for deception match its weaponry.

refs.note: The tongue as a fire of wickedness echoes the psalmist's complaint against lying lips.
A false witness is like a war club, sword, or sharp arrow—matching the psalm's warrior imagery.
Liars are excluded from the new Jerusalem, vindicating the psalmist's longing for truth.

hist.context: Psalm 120 opens the Songs of Ascents (120-134), a collection sung by pilgrims ascending to Jerusalem for the three annual festivals. The psalmist is stranded among hostile foreigners—Meshech (far north, near the Black Sea) and Kedar (Arabian desert)—longing for peace among those who love war. This displacement sets up the pilgrimage theme: the journey home to Zion from spiritual and geographical exile.

sections.header: Verses 5–7 — “Too Long Have I Lived Among Those Who Hate Peace”

heb.paragraph: "I am for peace (shālōm); but when I speak, they are for war" (v.7). The pilgrim’s lament: too long among the hostile (v.5). Meshek and Kedar (v.5) represent distant, foreign places — the exile’s location. The pilgrim is a peace-seeker surrounded by war-makers. The psalm’s final word is "war" — the pilgrimage is AWAY from war and TOWARD the shalom of Jerusalem (whose name contains shālōm).

notes.note: "Woe to me that I dwell in Meshek, that I live among the tents of Kedar! Too long have I lived among those who hate peace." Exile defined: living among the hostile. The pilgrimage is the journey from Meshek to Jerusalem, from war to peace.
"I am for peace; but when I speak, they are for war." The irreconcilable difference: the pilgrim offers peace; the neighbours choose war. There is no resolution except departure — toward Zion.

refs.note: Blessed are the peacemakers—the psalmist is for peace but surrounded by war lovers.
Live at peace with everyone as far as it depends on you, though some reject peace.
Seek peace and pursue it, even when others make peace impossible.

hist.context: The sharp arrows and burning coals (v4) invoke curse imagery—may the liar receive what he inflicts. Meshech and Kedar are geographically opposite extremes, suggesting universal hostility wherever the faithful dwell outside Zion. The brevity of this psalm (7 verses) matches its emotional intensity. It captures the pilgrim's cry before the journey begins: deliver me from this hostile world.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in I Call on the LORD in My Distress.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in I Call on the LORD in My Distress.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Solomon
ppl.text: Solomon is not a key figure in Psalm 120

ppl.name: Abraham
ppl.text: Abraham is not a key figure in Psalm 120

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.text: Jacob is not mentioned in Psalm 120. The psalm is about the psalmist's distress among deceitful people and hostile nations.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHe will punish you with a warrior’s sharp arrows, with burning coals of the broom bush.ESVA warrior\u2019s sharp arrows, with glowing coals of the broom tree!NIVWhat will he do to you, and what more besides, you deceitful tongue?ESVWhat shall be given to you, and what more shall be done to you, you deceitful tongue?

rec.text: ""I call on the LORD in my distress. Save me from lying lips." The pilgrimage begins in distress and deception. The journey to Zion is an escape from lies."

rows.label: Distress and Lies — 120:1–4
rows.text: First Song of Ascents. The pilgrimage starts in exile. "Save me from lying lips."

hebtext.gloss: song of ascents / song of the steps
hebtext.note: "A song of ascents (hammaʿaălōṭ)" (superscription). The first of fifteen Songs of Ascents (120–134), sung by pilgrims ascending to Jerusalem for the three annual festivals. Psalm 120 is the pilgrim’s starting point — still in exile, longing for Zion. "I call on the LORD in my distress, and he answers me" (v.1). "Save me, LORD, from lying lips and from deceitful tongues" (v.2). The deceitful tongue is compared to a warrior’s sharp arrows and burning coals (vv.3–4).

"I am for peace (shālōm); but when I speak, they are for war" (v.7). The pilgrim’s lament: too long among the hostile (v.5). Meshek and Kedar (v.5) represent distant, foreign places — the exile’s location. The pilgrim is a peace-seeker surrounded by war-makers. The psalm’s final word is "war" — the pilgrimage is AWAY from war and TOWARD the shalom of Jerusalem (whose name contains shālōm).

themes.note: The first Song of Ascents. The pilgrimage begins in exile, among lies and war. The journey toward Zion’s shalom starts here.
